articles.title: Stephen Colbert Signs Off the Late Show for the Last Time
articles.summary: Stephen Colbert hosted the final episode of The Late Show with Stephen Colbert on CBS tonight, May 21, 2026, closing out an 11-year run and ending a franchise that dates back 33 years. CBS cancelled the show in July 2025, calling it a financial decision, though many fans believe politics played a role. The finale caps a farewell week packed with guests including Jon Stewart, Steven Spielberg, and Bruce Springsteen.
articles.content:
Tonight is the last time Stephen Colbert will walk out onto that Ed Sullivan Theater stage. The Late Show with Stephen Colbert aired its final episode on CBS on Thursday, May 21, 2026, at 11:35 p.m. ET, also streaming live on Paramount+. After 11 seasons under Colbert, the Late Show franchise is officially gone forever.

The cancellation came out of nowhere for most fans. CBS announced in July 2025 that the show would end when Colbert's contract expired, describing it as a "purely a financial decision against a challenging backdrop in late night." But that explanation landed awkwardly. The Late Show had been the highest-rated American late-night talk show for nine consecutive seasons as of 2025. Unnamed sources at CBS said the show was losing $40 million per year with a staff of 200, with Colbert earning $20 million annually. Many fans doubted the financial story, believing the timing was connected to Paramount's merger with Skydance Media and the company's decision to settle a lawsuit filed by President Trump against CBS News.

The final week went out in style. Monday featured new comedy material, Tuesday brought Jon Stewart and Steven Spielberg plus a special performance by David Byrne and Stephen Colbert, and Wednesday featured Bruce Springsteen alongside a special edition of "The Colbert Questionert," a beloved recurring Q&A segment on the show. Even David Letterman, Colbert's predecessor who hosted Late Show for 22 years, stopped by last week to say goodbye, saying "I have every right to be pissed off" about the cancellation. Colbert himself took a different tone, going out with gratitude and plenty of jokes at CBS and Paramount's expense.

Colbert's biggest concern throughout the farewell run has been his 200 staff members who will now be out of work. As for what comes next for Colbert, the clearest answer involves hobbits. He confirmed he is co-writing a new Lord of the Rings movie for Warner Bros. alongside his son, Peter McGee, with Peter Jackson attached as director. The project was actually in the works before CBS pulled the plug. Colbert has also left the door open to creating a new show someday, though he says he hasn't been ready to seriously consider offers while finishing The Late Show. Starting May 22, Comics Unleashed takes over the 11:35 p.m. slot on CBS.
keyPoints: The Late Show with Stephen Colbert aired its final episode tonight, May 21, 2026, ending a 33-year CBS franchise
CBS cited financial losses of $40 million per year as the reason for cancellation despite the show being the top-rated late-night program for 9 straight seasons
The farewell week included guests Jon Stewart, Steven Spielberg, Bruce Springsteen, and David Letterman
Colbert is co-writing a new Lord of the Rings film for Warner Bros. with his son and Peter Jackson
About 200 Late Show staff members are now out of work following the finale
articles.wikiSearchQuery: The Late Show with Stephen Colbert CBS

articles.title: Meta Cuts 8,000 Jobs While Posting Record $56B Revenue
articles.summary: Meta began notifying roughly 8,000 employees on May 20 that they are being laid off, representing about 10% of its global workforce, as CEO Mark Zuckerberg redirects billions toward AI infrastructure. The layoffs arrive the same week Meta reported record quarterly revenue of $56.31 billion and net income of $26.8 billion, making the cuts a striking symbol of how Big Tech is reshaping itself around AI. An additional 7,000 workers are being reassigned to new AI-focused teams, and Meta is also cancelling 6,000 open job requisitions.
articles.content:
Meta started sending layoff emails to around 8,000 employees this week, and the rollout was as blunt as it sounds. Singapore-based workers were the first to find out, with notifications arriving at 4 a.m. local time. Employees in the UK and US were told to work from home as their mornings began and the emails landed. The cuts amount to roughly 10% of Meta's total workforce, which stood at just under 80,000 people at the end of March.

What makes this story genuinely jarring is the timing. These layoffs are happening the same week Meta reported record quarterly revenue of $56.31 billion and net income of $26.8 billion. Meta is not cutting jobs because it is struggling. It is cutting jobs because it has decided that AI infrastructure is worth more than the people it currently employs. Meta's projected capital expenditure for 2026 runs between $125 billion and $145 billion, more than double what it spent in 2025. Bank of America estimates the layoffs could generate $7 billion to $8 billion in annualised savings, money that will effectively flow straight into GPU farms and data centres.

Separate from the people being let go, Meta Chief People Officer Janelle Gale announced that around 7,000 workers will be redirected into newly created AI-focused teams, including groups called Applied AI Engineering and Agent Transformation Accelerator XFN. The company is also cancelling 6,000 open job requisitions, bringing the effective reduction in headcount to 14,000 positions. Engineering and product divisions are reportedly bearing a disproportionate share of the cuts. Inside Meta, the mood has not been good. Workers protested ahead of the announcement by hanging flyers on office walls and flooding internal channels with elephant graphics to signal the tension that leadership was not addressing.

Meta has indicated further cuts are possible later in the year, with additional rounds potentially coming in August and the autumn, though no firm scope has been set. Performance reviews at Meta now apparently factor in whether employees are actively using AI tools, which workers have described as a pressure to embrace the very technology replacing their colleagues. Meta is not alone in this pattern. The broader 2026 tech layoff wave has cut more than 113,000 jobs across the industry since January. The paradox of record profits and mass layoffs has become the defining story of the AI era in Big Tech.
keyPoints: Meta laid off roughly 8,000 employees on May 20, about 10% of its global workforce
The cuts arrived the same week Meta posted record quarterly revenue of $56.31 billion
An additional 7,000 workers are being moved into new AI-focused teams including Applied AI Engineering
Meta is also cancelling 6,000 open job requisitions, making the total effective headcount reduction 14,000
Further rounds of cuts are possible in August and the autumn, with 2026 AI infrastructure spending projected at up to $145 billion

articles.title: Musk's SpaceX 'Marshot' Pay Only Unlocks If Mars Gets a Million Residents
articles.summary: SpaceX's S-1 IPO filing has revealed a pay package for Elon Musk called 'Marshot' that is described as the largest in corporate history, built around a condition that reads more like science fiction than a legal document: a permanent human colony on Mars with at least one million inhabitants. The package involves a grant of 1 billion restricted shares that could be worth hundreds of billions of dollars, but only vest if SpaceX also hits a market capitalisation milestone of $7.5 trillion. It is the latest chapter in a string of blockbuster pay structures for Musk, following his $55.8 billion Tesla deal that ended up in a Delaware court.
articles.content:
Most CEOs get paid in stock options tied to quarterly earnings. Elon Musk has never been most CEOs. SpaceX's long-awaited IPO filing, published this week, has confirmed a pay structure so audacious it barely sounds real.

Elon Musk's new pay package at SpaceX, described as the largest in corporate history, comes with one little catch: he does not get the money until one million people live on Mars. SpaceX's board approved a grant of 1 billion performance-based restricted shares for Musk as part of a compensation plan tied not only to valuation milestones, but also to establishing a permanent human colony on Mars with at least one million inhabitants. The new shares, potentially worth an additional $600 billion or more, only vest if SpaceX hits two conditions: its top market capitalisation milestone of $7.5 trillion, and the creation of that permanent human Mars colony.

The filing says the award vests in 15 equal tranches tied to specified market-cap targets, and a second condition that reads more like science fiction than securities law. There is also a separate component. The filing outlines an award of about 302 million shares tied to orbital data centres that deliver 100 terawatts of compute a year, along with a SpaceX market-cap milestone of about $6.56 trillion. To understand why SpaceX is going public now, you need to understand what it has become. Three months before filing, Musk merged his AI company xAI and his social media platform X into SpaceX, in a deal that valued the rocket company at $1 trillion and the AI company at $250 billion. The idea, as the filing shows, is that the combined company can use Starlink's revenue plus SpaceX's launch business to subsidise the AI buildout, and use xAI's technology to make Mars actually governable at scale.

At the lower end of estimates, Musk's SpaceX pay package would still dwarf his $55.8 billion Tesla compensation plan that became the subject of a prolonged Delaware court battle. That Tesla saga involved shareholders suing to claw back the pay on the grounds the board was not independent enough. The SpaceX version raises the bar even further. The combined company posted a $4.3 billion net loss in the first quarter alone, with the drag coming almost entirely from xAI, which was folded into SpaceX in the February merger.

Not everyone is buying the vision. NASA advisor and Johns Hopkins research scientist Paul Sutter wrote in Scientific American that Musk's Mars timeline does not correspond to a real plan, comparing it to "announcing a camping trip on your next available weekend" without any supplies, with a car that is in the shop. "And has exploded." The Mars timeline itself is estimated on a range from multi-decades to never. SpaceX is targeting a $1.75 trillion valuation in its IPO. The public will soon be asked to decide how much they believe in the plan too.
keyPoints: SpaceX's IPO filing reveals the 'Marshot' pay package granting Elon Musk 1 billion restricted shares
The shares only vest if SpaceX hits a $7.5 trillion market cap AND a million-person Mars colony is established
A separate 302 million-share award is tied to orbital data centres delivering 100 terawatts of compute
SpaceX merged xAI and X into the company three months before filing, posting a $4.3 billion Q1 net loss
Scientists and NASA advisors say Musk's Mars timeline does not correspond to a realistic plan

articles.title: OpenAI Solves Paul Erdős's 1946 Math Problem — Verified This Time
articles.summary: OpenAI says an internal general-purpose reasoning model has disproved the Erdős unit distance conjecture, a geometry problem that went unsolved for nearly 80 years. This time, the claim is backed by independent mathematicians — including Fields medalist Tim Gowers, who said he would have accepted the proof in the Annals of Mathematics. The announcement is a direct redemption arc from a humiliating false claim OpenAI made just seven months ago.
articles.content:
OpenAI is claiming a genuine mathematical breakthrough, and this time the math community is nodding along. The company says an internal reasoning model has disproved the Erdős unit distance conjecture, a famous geometry problem first posed by the legendary Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős in 1946 that had stumped the world's best mathematicians for nearly 80 years.

The problem itself sounds almost too simple. It asks: if you scatter points on a flat plane, how many pairs of those points can sit exactly one unit apart? For decades, the assumption was that the answer was best illustrated by a square grid pattern. Erdős conjectured that square grids were essentially the optimal approach, and no one had managed to prove him wrong. OpenAI's reasoning model found an infinite family of point arrangements that do better than any square grid, delivering what mathematicians describe as a polynomial improvement over the previous best result.

What makes this especially notable is how the model did it. The proof came from a general-purpose reasoning model — not a system specially built for mathematics or trained to hunt for geometry solutions. It brought tools from algebraic number theory to bear on an elementary geometry question, which is the kind of creative cross-disciplinary leap that mathematicians genuinely find surprising. Independent mathematicians Noga Alon, Melanie Wood, and Thomas Bloom all checked the work and supported the result. Fields medalist Tim Gowers wrote a companion paper on the proof and said the result was one he would have accepted for publication in the Annals of Mathematics without hesitation — a remarkable endorsement from one of the most decorated mathematicians alive.

None of this would land quite the same way without the backstory. Seven months ago, former OpenAI VP Kevin Weil posted on X claiming that GPT-5 had found solutions to 10 previously unsolved Erdős problems. It turned out GPT-5 had simply rediscovered solutions that already existed in published mathematical literature. Critics including Yann LeCun and Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis publicly called it out, and Weil deleted the post.

So the credibility stakes here are high, and OpenAI clearly knew that. The decision to get external verification before making the announcement suggests the company learned its lesson. OpenAI researcher Noam Brown noted that less than a year ago, frontier AI models were performing at International Mathematical Olympiad gold level — and that the pace of progress suggests this kind of result will not be a one-off.

The broader implication is not just about one elegant geometry problem. OpenAI says its reasoning model is general-purpose, meaning the same ability to hold long chains of logic and bridge across disciplines could eventually be pointed at problems in biology, physics, or medicine. Whether or not you think AI is coming for pure mathematics, a proof that Fields medalists are willing to put their names on is a hard thing to dismiss.
keyPoints: OpenAI's internal reasoning model disproved the Erdős unit distance conjecture, a geometry problem unsolved since 1946
The proof was independently verified by mathematicians Noga Alon, Melanie Wood, and Thomas Bloom
Fields medalist Tim Gowers wrote a companion paper and said he would have accepted the proof for the Annals of Mathematics
The model used ideas from algebraic number theory to solve a geometry problem — it was not specially built for math
Seven months ago OpenAI falsely claimed GPT-5 had solved Erdős problems, making this verified result a significant redemption

articles.title: The Boys Ends After 5 Seasons and the Internet Is Losing It
articles.summary: The Boys officially wrapped on Prime Video on May 20, 2026, with its series finale titled "Blood and Bone," ending five seasons of superhero satire that made it one of the most talked-about shows on television. The finale put Karl Urban's Billy Butcher and Antony Starr's Homelander in a final collision, with creator Eric Kripke confirming Homelander meets his end stripped of power. Fan reactions are wildly split, but no one can stop talking about it.
articles.content:
After five seasons of blood, betrayal, and blistering superhero satire, The Boys has officially come to a close on Prime Video. The series finale, titled "Blood and Bone," dropped on May 20, 2026, a day after a 4DX theatrical premiere on May 19 at chains including Regal, AMC, and Marcus. The show, created by Eric Kripke and based on Garth Ennis and Darick Robertson's comic book series, launched in 2019 and became one of Amazon Prime Video's biggest hits.

At the heart of the finale is the conflict that has been building since episode one: Billy Butcher, played by Karl Urban, versus Homelander, played by Antony Starr. Season 5's core story had revolved around a race to acquire the last known vial of V-One, the original version of Compound V that renders Supes who take it invincible and immortal. Soldier Boy handed the vial to Homelander late in the season, making him the most powerful living Supe on the planet before the final showdown. Kripke told Deadline that Butcher and Hughie's conflict was "the secret conflict of the show" from the start, which made the finale feel so satisfying to write.

As for Homelander's fate, Kripke was direct: "He cannot walk out of that room alive." The whole season had been building to one final idea about power: take it away and there's nothing left underneath. Kripke described Homelander as "cowardly and blubbering and pathetic" when stripped of his abilities and faced with death, calling it a deliberate commentary on authoritarianism. The finale also squeezed in one last satirical target, a character called "the Disruptor" whose hat reads "Make America Super Again," written in black on black. Hughie and Annie got what Kripke called a "hopeful" ending. The world of The Boys is left permanently altered, with corrupt systems still standing but the main battle finally over.

Fan reactions have been all over the map. Some called it the best ending the show could have had. Others found it anticlimactic, and the whole season has taken heat for leaning harder into one-sided political commentary than earlier seasons did. Spinoff series Vought Rising is already filmed and set to arrive on Prime Video in 2027, with another spinoff, The Boys: Mexico, in development. The franchise is not going away. But The Boys as audiences have known it since 2019 is done, and the internet is absolutely not over it.
keyPoints: The Boys series finale "Blood and Bone" dropped on Prime Video on May 20, 2026, ending the show after 5 seasons
Homelander is killed in the finale, stripped of his powers and described by creator Eric Kripke as cowardly when faced with death
Butcher versus Hughie was confirmed by Kripke as "the secret conflict of the show" from the very beginning
Fan reactions are divided, with some praising the ending and others finding it anticlimactic
Spinoff Vought Rising arrives on Prime Video in 2027, with The Boys: Mexico also in development
articles.wikiSearchQuery: The Boys season 5 Prime Video series finale

articles.title: Mango Heir Jonathan Andic Arrested Over Father Isak Andic's Cliff Death
articles.summary: Jonathan Andic, the eldest son of Mango fashion empire founder Isak Andic, was arrested on May 19 in connection with his father's death, which is now being investigated as a homicide. Isak Andic died in December 2024 after falling 320 feet into a ravine while hiking near Barcelona with Jonathan, who was the only witness. Jonathan has since posted €1 million bail and the family says he is cooperating fully and maintains his innocence.
articles.content:
This story took a dramatic turn this week. Jonathan Andic, the eldest son of Mango founder Isak Andic and the current vice chairman of Mango's board, was arrested on Tuesday in connection with his father's death. The case is now officially being investigated as a homicide by the High Court of Justice of Barcelona.

The backstory: in December 2024, Isak Andic, who was 71 years old, died after plunging 320 feet down a ravine while hiking near Barcelona. Jonathan was the only person present. Police initially treated the incident as an accident and even briefly closed the case in early 2025, before reopening it in March of that year. Nearly 18 months later, Jonathan now finds himself under arrest.

Isak Andic was one of the most significant figures in European fashion. Born in Turkey, he opened Mango's first store in Barcelona in 1984 and built it into a global giant with 2,900 stores across 120 markets. Mango posted sales of €3.8 billion last year. It stands alongside Inditex-owned Zara as one of the dominant forces in Spanish fashion. Jonathan and his two sisters inherited the bulk of the company, holding a combined 95% stake, with CEO Toni Ruiz owning the remaining 5%.

After appearing in court in Martorell, just outside Barcelona, Jonathan paid a bail of €1 million, which the High Court of Justice of Barcelona confirmed had already been settled. He has also been ordered to surrender his passport and to appear in court weekly while the investigation continues. A family spokesperson told Reuters that Jonathan is cooperating fully with authorities and that the family is confident of his innocence. The case has shocked the fashion world and raised serious questions about what really happened on that hillside in December 2024.
keyPoints: Jonathan Andic, son of Mango founder Isak Andic, was arrested on May 19 and the case is being investigated as a homicide
Isak Andic died in December 2024 after falling 320 feet into a ravine near Barcelona while hiking with Jonathan, who was the only witness
Police initially ruled it an accident before reopening the case in March 2025
Jonathan posted €1 million bail and has been ordered to surrender his passport and appear in court weekly
Mango is one of Spain's biggest fashion retailers, with 2,900 stores in 120 markets and €3.8 billion in annual sales

articles.title: Netflix Drops Michael Jackson: The Verdict Trailer After Biopic Ignored Abuse Claims
articles.summary: Netflix has released the first trailer for Michael Jackson: The Verdict, a three-part docuseries exploring Michael Jackson's 2005 child molestation trial, premiering June 3. The move comes directly on the heels of Antoine Fuqua's biopic Michael, which has crossed $700 million at the worldwide box office but drew criticism for skipping over Jackson's abuse allegations entirely. The docuseries features jurors, witnesses, accusers, and defenders who were inside the courtroom.
articles.content:
Netflix has dropped the first trailer for Michael Jackson: The Verdict, a three-part docuseries that goes exactly where this year's blockbuster biopic refused to go. The series premieres June 3, 2026, and it is squarely aimed at the most controversial chapter of Michael Jackson's life.

Here is the quick background. In 2003, Jackson was charged with multiple counts of child molestation involving 13-year-old cancer patient Gavin Arvizo. The charges also included administering alcohol to a minor and conspiracy to commit child abduction and false imprisonment, totalling 10 criminal counts. The trial became a full-blown media circus and ended on June 13, 2005, with Jackson being acquitted on all 10 counts. No cameras were allowed inside the courtroom, which means the public only ever got a filtered, piecemeal view of what actually happened. Jackson died in 2009, but the controversy never went away.

This year, director Antoine Fuqua's biopic Michael reignited public fascination with the pop star. Starring Jackson's real-life nephew Jaafar Jackson in the title role alongside Colman Domingo, Nia Long, and Miles Teller, the film follows Jackson's life from his days with the Jackson 5 through his Bad tour in 1988. It deliberately stops before the abuse allegations and has already crossed $700 million worldwide, making it one of the biggest films of 2026. But critics and audiences alike called it out for leaving the darker parts of Jackson's story completely untouched.

Netflix is stepping directly into that gap. Michael Jackson: The Verdict is directed by Nick Green and executive produced by Fiona Stourton, David Herman, and former ABC News president James Goldston. Each of the three episodes runs 50 minutes. The doc features jurors, eyewitnesses, media members who covered the trial, and both accusers and defenders, presenting the case from the prosecution and defense sides equally. The trailer includes Macaulay Culkin, the Home Alone star who was a close friend of Jackson's and who emphatically denied that Jackson had done anything wrong. Another voice in the trailer goes the other way entirely, saying "We believed he was a criminal and he was able to get away with it because of his fame and celebrity." That tension is exactly what the docuseries promises to put under the microscope starting June 3.
keyPoints: Netflix's Michael Jackson: The Verdict is a three-part docuseries premiering June 3, 2026
It covers Jackson's 2003 charges, 2005 trial, and acquittal on all 10 counts
The docuseries fills the gap left by the biopic Michael, which ended its story in 1988 and ignored the abuse allegations
The biopic has crossed $700 million worldwide and is one of 2026's biggest box office hits
The doc features jurors, accusers, defenders, and Macaulay Culkin, giving both prosecution and defense perspectives
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Michael Jackson The Verdict Netflix docuseries 2026

articles.title: Southampton Spygate Appeal Fails: Hull City Face Boro at Wembley
articles.summary: Southampton's appeal against their expulsion from the Sky Bet Championship play-offs has been dismissed, meaning Hull City will face Middlesbrough in the play-off final at Wembley Stadium on Saturday, May 23 at 3:30pm. Southampton were thrown out after admitting their staff secretly filmed opponents' training sessions on three separate occasions. The decision is final and cannot be appealed further.
articles.content:
It is over for Southampton. Their last shot at reversing one of the most dramatic punishments in English football history has been rejected, and the club's season is done in the most painful way imaginable.

Here is what happened. Middlesbrough initially reported Southampton to the EFL when they alleged a Southampton staff member had spied on a training session on May 7, ahead of their play-off semi-final, in breach of league rules. It then emerged the problem was bigger than one incident. Southampton admitted breaches of EFL regulations requiring clubs to act with the utmost good faith and prohibiting the observation of another club's training session within 72 hours of a scheduled match. The admitted breaches concern fixtures against Oxford United in December 2025, Ipswich Town in April 2026, and Middlesbrough in May 2026. All three incidents occurred following the appointment of Tonda Eckert as head coach in early December.

Southampton were expelled from the Championship play-offs, with Middlesbrough reinstated after a bombshell verdict from an independent commission. In addition, Southampton were handed a four-point deduction for next season. Southampton quickly appealed, but on Wednesday evening the answer came back. The original sanction remains in force. Southampton's expulsion from the Sky Bet Championship Play-Offs stands, alongside the four-point deduction for the 2026/27 season. In accordance with EFL regulations, the decision of the League Arbitration Panel is final. The Sky Bet Championship Play-Off Final between Hull City and Middlesbrough is confirmed as taking place at Wembley Stadium on Saturday 23 May, kicking off at 3:30pm.

The human cost of this is enormous. More than 37,000 Southampton fans had already bought tickets for the final and will now be seeking refunds. Southampton's spying scandal, which they have admitted to, could ultimately cost them a place in the Premier League and an estimated £200 million. Head coach Tonda Eckert, 33, may now also face separate action from the Football Association, according to reports. Southampton released a statement calling it "a painful moment" and vowing to "respond with humility, accountability and determination to put things right." Meanwhile, Middlesbrough are now focused entirely on their game against Hull City at Wembley on Saturday. For Hull and Boro, the prize is Premier League promotion. For Southampton, a season of promise has ended in scandal.
keyPoints: Southampton's appeal against play-off expulsion has been dismissed and cannot be appealed further
Hull City will face Middlesbrough in the Championship play-off final at Wembley on Saturday 23 May at 3:30pm
Southampton admitted to illegally filming training sessions of Oxford United, Ipswich Town, and Middlesbrough
Southampton also received a four-point deduction for the 2026/27 season
Head coach Tonda Eckert could face further action from the Football Association

articles.title: James Murdoch Buys New York Magazine Back Into the Family
articles.summary: James Murdoch's investment firm Lupa Systems has agreed to acquire New York Magazine, the Vox Media Podcast Network, and Vox.com from Vox Media in a deal worth more than $300 million. The move gives the more moderate Murdoch son a serious foothold in culture and journalism, and it brings New York Magazine back under Murdoch ownership for the first time since 1991. Vox CEO Jim Bankoff will stay on to run the acquired assets, and the deal is expected to close within four to six weeks.
articles.content:
James Murdoch just made the biggest media bet of his post-Fox career. His holding company Lupa Systems has agreed to buy New York Magazine, including its offshoots The Cut and Vulture, the Vox Media Podcast Network, and Vox.com from Vox Media. The New York Times puts the price tag at more than $300 million.

Here is the twist that makes the deal genuinely weird in the best way. James's father, Rupert Murdoch, owned New York Magazine from the late 1970s until he sold it in 1991. So the magazine is literally returning to a Murdoch for the first time in 35 years. The difference is that James is widely seen as a very different kind of media owner. He resigned from News Corp's board back in 2020 over what he called disagreements about editorial content, specifically the company's climate denial coverage. He and his siblings later settled a family legal dispute that left his brother Lachlan in control of Fox Corp and News Corp, effectively cutting James out of the family empire entirely.

The podcast side of this deal is no afterthought. Vox's podcast network includes Pivot, the hugely popular tech and business show co-hosted by Kara Swisher and Scott Galloway. Swisher said of the new owner, "Unlike many other media owners these days, they're savvy about the business and willing to take smart risks." Galloway went further, saying, "James is the only Murdoch that this deal could have happened with." Pivot still has three years left on its contract and will continue under Lupa Systems.

Not everything at Vox Media is changing hands. Properties including The Verge, Eater, Popsugar, SB Nation, and The Dodo are not part of the deal. Those will spin off into a separate independent company under a yet-to-be-named corporate brand, led by current Vox Media president Ryan Pauley. Meanwhile, Vox Media CEO Jim Bankoff will join Lupa Systems and continue running the acquired assets. In a statement, James Murdoch said the acquisition reflects his interest in "the forward edge of culture" and a commitment to "ambitious journalism." The deal is expected to close in four to six weeks.
keyPoints: James Murdoch's Lupa Systems is buying New York Magazine, the Vox Media Podcast Network, and Vox.com for more than $300 million
This brings New York Magazine back under Murdoch family ownership for the first time since Rupert Murdoch sold it in 1991
Vox CEO Jim Bankoff stays on to run the assets under Lupa Systems
The Verge, Eater, SB Nation, Popsugar, and The Dodo are not included and will form a separate independent company
Kara Swisher and Scott Galloway's Pivot podcast is part of the deal, with three years remaining on its contract
articles.wikiSearchQuery: New York Magazine Vox Media acquisition

articles.title: North Korea's Naegohyang FC Beats South Korea on South Korean Soil
articles.summary: North Korea's Naegohyang Women's FC beat South Korea's Suwon FC Women 2-1 in Suwon to reach the Asian Women's Champions League final, becoming the first North Korean sports team to visit the South in eight years. The match was a rare and deeply symbolic moment on the Korean Peninsula, where the two countries have technically remained at war since the 1950s. Naegohyang will now face Japan's Tokyo Verdy Beleza in Saturday's final at the same stadium.
articles.content:
Sport does not always change the world, but every now and then it produces a moment that feels genuinely historic. On Wednesday in Suwon, South Korea, that moment arrived in the form of a football match.

North Korea's first sports team to visit the South in eight years reached the Asian Women's Champions League final as Naegohyang Women's FC beat Suwon FC Women 2-1. The Asian Women's Champions League is the top club competition in women's football across Asia, run by the Asian Football Confederation. Getting there required something almost unthinkable given the frozen state of relations between Pyongyang and Seoul: a North Korean team physically crossing into South Korea to play a competitive match.

In a rare breakthrough after years of frozen relations, North Korea's women's soccer team arrived in South Korea for the first time in over seven years. According to Seoul's Unification Ministry, the visiting delegation consisted of 39 people, including 27 players and 12 staff members. The interest from South Korean fans was immediate. Interest in the rare North-South semifinal was intense, and 7,087 general admission tickets sold out within hours of going on sale last week.

Naegohyang came from behind to defeat their rivals from the South amid torrential rain in Suwon, in a hard-fought semifinal played in a fair spirit without any major flashpoints. The North Koreans equalised six minutes after going behind when Choe headed home from a free kick, and prolific striker Kim Kyong Yong then gave Naegohyang the lead when she finished off a scrappy attack with a header in the 67th minute. Suwon captain Ji So-yun, a former Chelsea midfielder, missed a penalty with just over 10 minutes to play, and that miss proved decisive.

The North Koreans hugged each other and wept at the full-time whistle as the Suwon players slumped to the turf. The North Koreans will stay in the South for Saturday's final, where they will take on Japan's Tokyo Verdy Beleza at the same stadium. Back home, the reaction from state media was notably muted. North Korea's state dispatch omitted several details surrounding the match, including the South Korean crowd's reception of the visiting team and the torrential rain that drenched the pitch. The Rodong Sinmun, the official paper of the ruling Workers' Party, did run photos of players competing in the downpour, but South Korean national flags on the players' uniforms were blurred.
keyPoints: Naegohyang Women's FC beat Suwon FC Women 2-1 to reach the Asian Women's Champions League final
This is the first North Korean sports team to visit South Korea in eight years
Striker Kim Kyong Yong scored the winning header in the 67th minute
Naegohyang will face Japan's Tokyo Verdy Beleza in Saturday's final
North Korean state media reported the result but blurred South Korean flags from published photos

articles.title: Granta Published a Prize-Winning Story That May Be AI-Written
articles.summary: A short story by Jamir Nazir called "The Serpent in the Grove" won a regional prize in the 2026 Commonwealth Short Story Prize and was published in Granta, one of the most prestigious literary magazines in the world — and readers almost immediately suspected it was written by AI. An AI detection tool called Pangram flagged the story as 100% AI-generated, and the scandal has since grown to potentially involve three of the five winning stories. The Commonwealth Foundation has now launched a formal review of its selection process.
articles.content:
Here is the short version: a story beat out nearly 8,000 other entries to win a slot in Granta, one of the most celebrated literary magazines on the planet, and a lot of people think a chatbot wrote it.

The story in question is "The Serpent in the Grove" by Jamir Nazir. It was published in Granta as the Caribbean regional winner of the 2026 Commonwealth Short Story Prize — a competition that recognizes unpublished fiction from writers across countries that used to make up the British Commonwealth. The judges, chaired by award-winning novelist Louise Doughty, praised the story's "precise yet richly evocative" language. It beat out 7,806 total entries across all regions to make the final five.

Then the internet got involved. Ethan Mollick, a Wharton professor who studies AI's effects on the workplace, ran the story through Pangram, an AI detection program that claims 99% accuracy. The results flagged the story as fully AI-generated. And it did not stop there. Pangram also flagged "The Bastion's Shadow" by Maltese writer John Edward DeMicoli as fully AI-generated, and "Mehendi Nights" by Indian writer Sharon Aruparayil as partially AI-generated. Only the entries by Holly Ann Miller and Lisa-Anne Julien came back as fully human-written.

Granta said it tested the stories using Anthropic's Claude as an AI detection tool, but the results were inconclusive. The magazine has decided to keep the stories published on its website for now. The Commonwealth Foundation, for its part, announced it is conducting a "thorough, transparent review of the selection process."

There is also a layer of intrigue around Nazir himself. His bio identifies him as a 61-year-old Trinidadian writer, but he has almost no digital footprint outside of a self-published book of inspirational poems from 2018. His LinkedIn profile shows him as a frequent AI advocate. None of that proves anything on its own, but it adds to the noise.

What this story really exposes is a structural problem. Literary prizes like the Commonwealth Short Story Prize are built on human craft and human judgment. AI detection tools are not foolproof, and experts are not all in agreement. But the fact that three of five winning stories triggered red flags from the same tool is hard to ignore. The question now is not just whether these specific stories are AI-written. It is whether the literary world has any reliable way to find out.
keyPoints: Jamir Nazir's story 'The Serpent in the Grove' won the Caribbean region prize at the 2026 Commonwealth Short Story Prize and was published in Granta
AI detection tool Pangram flagged the story as 100% AI-generated
Three of the five winning stories across all regions were flagged by Pangram as fully or partially AI-written
Granta tested the stories with Anthropic's Claude but said results were inconclusive and kept the stories published
The Commonwealth Foundation has launched a formal review of how it selected winners

articles.title: Jeff Probst Spoiled Survivor 50's Fire-Making Result Live on Air
articles.summary: Jeff Probst accidentally revealed the loser of the Survivor 50 fire-making challenge live on CBS before the footage had even aired, leaving the studio audience gasping. Production staff prompted Probst to bring out eliminated castaway Rizo Velovic prematurely, spoiling the result for millions of viewers watching at home. Probst recovered with a joke, calling it "the last twist of the season," but the damage was already done.
articles.content:
Survivor 50 just wrapped up its landmark 50th season with a live finale, and Jeff Probst managed to make it truly unforgettable. Just not in the way anyone planned.

Jeff Probst accidentally revealed the winner and loser of the Survivor 50 fire-making competition before the segment aired. During Wednesday night's finale, Aubry Bracco won the final immunity challenge and chose to send Rizo Velovic and Jonathan Young to the fire-making competition. But after CBS aired footage of her announcing her decision, the episode cut to Probst onstage in front of a live studio audience in Los Angeles.

That is where things went sideways. When CBS cut to Probst in front of a live audience, the host appeared to mix up the production cues and mistakenly brought out Velovic onstage, announcing him as the loser before the challenge had been broadcast. The taped footage from Fiji had not yet aired for East Coast viewers at that point in the broadcast, meaning millions of fans learned Rizo's fate seconds before watching it happen. Fellow contestant Cirie Fields alerted Probst to the error, who exited the stage during the commercial break to regroup.

Probst handled it like a pro, more or less. "I love doing live television. In case you're confused, this is what happened," Probst said. "We were going to show you fire-making, and then have the loser of fire-making, Rizo, come out and talk about how charming he is. Instead, we did a Survivor twist. It's the last twist of the season. We called it a peak into the future. So now, we're gonna watch Rizo lose in fire to Jonathan." The episode then showed the fire-making challenge, where Young successfully sparked before Velovic, securing his spot in the final three.

Ultimately, it was Bracco who was named the winner of Survivor 50, winning the show with a total of eight jury votes. As for the live finale format itself, the accidental spoiler came during the three-hour live grand finale, the first live reunion broadcast for Survivor in a decade. Probst had accidentally spoiled an elimination for the first time in the show's history. Given the chaos, it may also be the last time the show attempts the format. As one viewer put it online, this will probably be the last live finale whether the blunder happened or not.
keyPoints: Jeff Probst accidentally revealed that Rizo Velovic lost the fire-making challenge before it aired
A production cue error caused Velovic to be brought onstage prematurely in front of a live LA audience
Probst joked it was "the last twist of the season" when he returned from a commercial break
Jonathan Young won the fire-making challenge, and Aubry Bracco won Survivor 50 with eight jury votes
This was Survivor's first live finale in a decade, and the first time Probst has ever accidentally spoiled an elimination

articles.title: Netflix Goes Live Daily with The Breakfast Club from June 1
articles.summary: Netflix and iHeartMedia announced on 21 May 2026 that The Breakfast Club, the hip-hop morning show hosted by Charlamagne Tha God, DJ Envy, Jess Hilarious, and Loren LoRosa, will stream live on Netflix every weekday starting June 1. This is Netflix's first ever live daily show, a major shift for a platform built on on-demand content. The stream will run for nearly three hours each morning and is available to subscribers worldwide.
articles.content:
Netflix just made a move that nobody in the streaming world saw coming. Starting June 1, 2026, The Breakfast Club will stream live on Netflix every single weekday morning. That makes it Netflix's first ever live daily show, and it is a genuinely big deal for what has always been a platform built around watching whatever you want, whenever you want.

The Breakfast Club, for anyone who does not know it, is the hip-hop and R&B morning show that originates from iHeartMedia's Power 105.1 radio station in New York. It launched in 2010 and has become one of the most influential shows in Black pop culture, hosting guests ranging from Barack Obama and Jay-Z to Kendrick Lamar and Cardi B. Charlamagne Tha God and the show were inducted into the Radio Hall of Fame in 2020. The current lineup features Charlamagne alongside co-hosts DJ Envy, Jess Hilarious, and Loren LoRosa.

So what will the Netflix version look like? Subscribers around the world will get almost three hours of uninterrupted programming each weekday. The regular radio broadcast still includes traditional ad breaks, but on Netflix those slots will be filled with exclusive bonus content including behind-the-scenes moments and extended conversations that radio listeners will not hear. The show's audio will continue on more than 100 broadcast radio stations nationwide and on the iHeartRadio app, so radio fans are not losing anything.

This is actually the next chapter of a partnership that has been building for a while. The video-podcast version of The Breakfast Club moved to Netflix back in January 2026, when full episodes stopped being distributed on YouTube. That shift frustrated some fans who were used to watching for free. The move to live streaming takes it a significant step further, turning a radio show into a genuine global television event.

Charlamagne summed up the ambition perfectly in his statement about the deal: "Mornings in New York. Daytime in the U.K. and Ghana. Evenings across other parts of the world." The idea of a New York morning radio show reaching people in real time across multiple continents is a real statement about where media is heading. iHeartMedia CEO Bob Pittman called The Breakfast Club "the center of culture," and with this Netflix deal, that claim just got a whole lot bigger.
keyPoints: The Breakfast Club will stream live on Netflix every weekday starting June 1, 2026
This is Netflix's first ever live daily show
The Netflix stream runs nearly three hours with exclusive bonus segments replacing ad breaks
Charlamagne Tha God, DJ Envy, Jess Hilarious, and Loren LoRosa host the show
Audio remains on 100-plus radio stations and the iHeartRadio app worldwide
articles.wikiSearchQuery: The Breakfast Club radio show Charlamagne Tha God

articles.title: Airbnb Now Books Hotels, Car Rentals and Uses AI for Everything
articles.summary: Airbnb has launched its biggest ever update, adding boutique hotels, car rentals, grocery delivery, and luggage storage to its app as CEO Brian Chesky pushes toward building what he calls an "Amazon for travel." The summer 2026 release also deepens AI across host onboarding, review summaries, and customer support — which now handles 40% of all queries. It's the clearest sign yet that Airbnb is no longer just a home-rental platform.
articles.content:
Airbnb just blew past its own identity. The app that made its name letting you sleep in a stranger's spare room is now booking you boutique hotels, picking you up from the airport, stocking your fridge, and storing your luggage. CEO Brian Chesky announced the summer 2026 release on Wednesday in San Francisco, and the scope of it is genuinely surprising.

Airbnb is launching hotel listings by partnering with boutique hotels in 20 cities, including New York, Paris, London, Madrid, Rome, and Singapore. These are not big chains. Airbnb is focusing specifically on boutique and independent hotels, each selected for its neighbourhood location, design, and hospitality rather than chain affiliation. Airbnb is also offering a price-match guarantee, promising to refund the difference in app credits if the same room is found cheaper elsewhere. Guests can earn up to 15% back in platform credits when booking boutique hotels.

Beyond hotels, Airbnb is layering on services to keep travellers inside the app for longer. Luggage storage is available through a partnership with Bounce across more than 15,000 locations in 175 cities. Car rentals launch this summer with 20% credit back on first bookings. These join the grocery delivery and airport pickup services Airbnb rolled out earlier this year. Chesky told CNBC's Andrew Ross Sorkin that he imagines Airbnb one day having "dozens, possibly even hundreds of categories, just like Amazon" and wants to build "an Amazon for services, at least for traveling and living."

The AI push is just as ambitious. Artificial intelligence is becoming a core layer of Airbnb's product strategy, particularly on the host side. One of the key updates allows hosts to input minimal information while AI automatically fills in listing details, significantly simplifying the onboarding process. A host can say "list my place," provide an address, and AI uses computer vision to write the description and infer amenities — turning a multi-hour process into five minutes. On the guest side, guests get AI-generated review summaries with category tags for location, amenities, and family-friendliness, plus a new comparison tool that shows AI-generated summaries of properties saved to a wishlist.

Customer service is another major area where Airbnb is deploying AI at scale. Its AI-powered support bot, initially launched in the US, already handles around 40% of customer queries, and Airbnb now plans to expand it globally with support for 11 languages. Later this year, Airbnb said it will start using a voice-based AI assistant that users can talk to on a call. The bigger picture here is strategic. The move is the most aggressive step yet in Airbnb's push to capture a larger share of travel spending that currently flows to competitors like Booking.com and Expedia. Airbnb posted Q1 2026 revenue of $2.68 billion, up 18% year over year — so Chesky has the war chest to pull this off.
keyPoints: Airbnb is adding boutique hotel listings in 20 cities including New York, Paris, and London
Car rentals, grocery delivery via Instacart, airport pickups, and luggage storage at 15,000+ locations are all coming this summer
AI now auto-fills host listing details from just an address, cutting onboarding from hours to five minutes
Airbnb's AI customer support bot handles 40% of queries and is expanding to 11 languages
A voice-based AI assistant for customer calls is planned for later in 2026

articles.title: Your Burger Habit Is Getting Really Expensive Really Fast
articles.summary: Beef prices in the United States have surged roughly 16% compared to last year, with ground beef now averaging nearly $7 a pound at the grocery store. A shrinking U.S. cattle herd, high consumer demand, and rising production costs are all pushing prices higher, and relief is not expected before 2028. The USDA forecasts beef prices could climb another 10% or more in 2026 alone.
articles.content:
If your grocery bill has been quietly shocking you at the checkout line, beef is a big reason why. Compared to last year, the cost of beef to consumers is up approximately 16% across the board in 2026, according to data from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. Ground beef is now averaging around $6.70 to nearly $7 a pound, up from roughly $3.96 per pound in 2021. Steaks have climbed even higher, hitting around $12.73 per pound. That is not a typo.

The core problem is simple economics. The U.S. cattle herd has fallen to its smallest size since the 1960s, with the number of beef cows dropping to less than 28 million in January, down from a peak of 94.7 million head in 2019. A combination of persistent drought, high interest rates, and rising production costs pushed ranchers to shrink their herds rather than expand them. Beef cow slaughter is down 17.4% year over year so far in 2026, marking the fourth straight year of double-digit declines. Meanwhile, American appetite for beef has barely flinched. Data from NielsenIQ shows that as of late March, unit sales for beef are down only 4% year-over-year, while dollar sales are up 8%.

Live cattle futures at the CME Group settled at $2.51 per pound in mid-April, the highest price on record going back to the 1960s according to FactSet data, and the contract has jumped more than 25% over the past 12 months. To help fill the gap between supply and demand, beef imports have surged, rising 18% year over year in 2025 and up another 13.3% in early 2026. But imports alone are not enough to bring prices down. On top of the supply crunch, rising energy prices linked to geopolitical tensions are pushing up transportation and production costs across the entire beef supply chain.

The USDA now estimates beef prices could climb more than 10% in 2026, with some projections reaching as high as 18%. Economists say the cattle herd is unlikely to rebuild meaningfully until at least 2028. Restaurants with heavy beef exposure, like McDonald's, Chipotle, Shake Shack, and Cracker Barrel, could face weaker sales as a result. The Trump administration has begun pointing fingers at meatpackers, with the Justice Department confirming it is investigating potential antitrust violations in the beef industry. At the same time, Tyson Foods, the country's biggest beef processor, reported another quarterly loss in its beef business, suggesting the big companies are not the ones pocketing the gains. The bottom line: summer barbecue season just got a lot more expensive, and there is no quick fix in sight.
keyPoints: Beef prices are up roughly 16% year-over-year in 2026, with ground beef averaging nearly $7 a pound
The U.S. cattle herd is at its smallest since the 1960s, down from a 2019 peak of 94.7 million head
The USDA forecasts beef prices could rise more than 10% in 2026, possibly as much as 18%
Cattle herd recovery is not expected before 2028, meaning high prices are here to stay
The Justice Department is investigating potential antitrust violations by meatpackers
articles.wikiSearchQuery: beef prices United States cattle herd 2026

articles.title: Brad Pitt's Cliff Booth Movie Hits IMAX First, Then Netflix
articles.summary: Netflix's The Adventures of Cliff Booth starring Brad Pitt will open in IMAX theaters on November 25 for two weeks before arriving on Netflix in December. It's a rare big-screen push from a streamer that usually skips theatrical runs entirely. The move signals Netflix is doubling down on the kind of event-film treatment that drives buzz and awards attention.
articles.content:
Brad Pitt is heading back to the big screen in a big way. Netflix confirmed Wednesday that The Adventures of Cliff Booth will open exclusively in IMAX theaters on November 25, Thanksgiving weekend, before making its way to Netflix in December.

If the name Cliff Booth rings a bell, that's because Brad Pitt first played the character in Quentin Tarantino's Once Upon a Time in Hollywood in 2019. Pitt won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor for that role, playing the cool, dangerous stuntman sidekick to Leonardo DiCaprio's fading TV star. The Adventures of Cliff Booth is a full standalone film built around that fan-favourite character.

The IMAX window is a notable move for Netflix. The streamer has typically favoured dropping films straight onto its platform, so giving The Adventures of Cliff Booth a dedicated two-week theatrical run on IMAX screens is a clear signal that Netflix wants this one to feel like a genuine cinema event. Thanksgiving weekend is one of the biggest moviegoing periods of the year, which means the film will be competing hard for attention alongside major studio releases.

For Brad Pitt fans, this is a treat. The Cliff Booth character was one of the most talked-about performances of 2019, and the idea of an entire film built around him is exactly the kind of follow-up that audiences have been quietly hoping for. Mark November 25 in your calendar.
keyPoints: The Adventures of Cliff Booth opens in IMAX theaters on November 25, Thanksgiving weekend
The film moves to Netflix in December after a two-week IMAX window
Brad Pitt first played Cliff Booth in Quentin Tarantino's Once Upon a Time in Hollywood in 2019
Pitt won the Best Supporting Actor Oscar for that original role
The theatrical release is a rare move for Netflix, signalling major event-film ambitions
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood 2019 film

articles.title: Djokovic, Sinner and Gauff to Walk Out of French Open Interviews
articles.summary: The world's top tennis players, including Novak Djokovic, Jannik Sinner, Aryna Sabalenka, and Coco Gauff, plan to walk out of Roland Garros press conferences after just 15 minutes this Friday and Saturday in protest at Grand Slam prize money. The 15-minute limit is a deliberate symbol: it mirrors the 15% of revenue that the four Grand Slams currently pay out to players. Players are demanding that figure rise to 22% by 2030.
articles.content:
Roland Garros has one of the most electric atmospheres in sport. But this week the tension off the court may outshine anything on it. The world's best tennis players are staging a coordinated media protest at the 2026 French Open, and they have a very specific number in mind: 15.

The world's leading tennis players plan to limit their pre-French Open media commitments to 15 minutes in protest at the prize money on offer. They could walk out of news conferences or broadcast interviews at Roland Garros this Friday and Saturday when the 15 minutes is up. The 15-minute limit is meant to symbolise the 15% of revenue which, broadly speaking, the Grand Slams allocate to prize money.

The leading 20 male and female players — a group that includes Novak Djokovic, Jannik Sinner, Aryna Sabalenka, and Coco Gauff — are leading the charge. Players will also refuse interviews with the French Open media and the tournament's primary rights holders, TNT Sports and Eurosport, though they will still conduct flash interviews with broadcasters after matches in order to avoid fines. The protest is being coordinated: it began in late 2025 and is being organised by former WTA chairman and chief executive Larry Scott, who will be in Paris on Friday to meet French Open tournament director Amélie Mauresmo and French Tennis Federation president Gilles Moretton. Further meetings are scheduled with representatives of the All England Club and the US Tennis Association during the fortnight.

The numbers behind the dispute tell a clear story. The French Open's 2026 prize pool is up 9.5% from 2025 to $72.3 million, which is about 15% of the tournament's projected revenue by the players' estimates. The annual prize money increase was 20% at last year's US Open and nearly 16% at January's Australian Open — so the French Open is already lagging behind its fellow Slams. Athletes are asking that by 2030, 22% of Grand Slam revenues be allocated to prize money, and also want tens of millions of dollars directed toward pensions, healthcare, and maternity benefits, as well as greater consultation on scheduling and other key decisions.

The French Tennis Federation has pushed back hard. The FFT said the players' decision to cut short media duties "penalises all stakeholders in the tournament — the media, broadcasters, the federation's staff, and the entire tennis family who enthusiastically follow each edition of Roland Garros." The players are unbothered. Women's world No. 1 Aryna Sabalenka said earlier this month at the Italian Open that players might have to boycott the sport's four biggest tournaments to "defend our rights." And Jannik Sinner earlier this month called for the Slam events to show "respect" to players in the row over prize money. The protest is symbolic for now. But with the French Open running from May 24 to June 7, and more meetings with the All England Club and US Tennis Association lined up, this dispute is not going away.
keyPoints: Top players including Djokovic, Sinner, Sabalenka, and Gauff will walk out of Roland Garros press conferences after 15 minutes
The 15-minute limit symbolises the 15% of revenue Grand Slams currently pay out as prize money
Players are demanding 22% of Grand Slam revenue go to prize money by 2030
The French Open's 2026 prize pool rose only 9.5%, compared to 20% at the US Open and 16% at the Australian Open
Former WTA CEO Larry Scott is coordinating the protest and will hold meetings at Roland Garros this week
articles.wikiSearchQuery: French Open Roland Garros prize money dispute 2026

articles.title: The Bear Earns Illinois Its Official State Sandwich: Italian Beef
articles.summary: The Illinois General Assembly officially named the Italian beef sandwich the state sandwich of Illinois on Wednesday, May 20, directly crediting The Bear as a reason for the dish's cultural resurgence. The Bear actor Corey Hendrix, a Chicago native who plays Gary "Sweeps" Woods on the FX and Hulu show, traveled to Springfield for the ceremony. The timing could not be better: The Bear's fifth and final season premieres on June 25.
articles.content:
A TV show just got a sandwich made into law. On Wednesday, the Illinois General Assembly passed an official resolution naming the Italian beef sandwich the state sandwich of Illinois, and The Bear got a shoutout right there in the legal text.

The Bear, for the uninitiated, is the critically acclaimed FX and Hulu drama created by Christopher Storer. It follows the chaotic, emotionally raw world of a family-owned Italian beef sandwich shop in Chicago called the Original Beef, which gradually transforms into a fine-dining restaurant. The show stars Jeremy Allen White, Ayo Edebiri, and Ebon Moss-Bachrach, and it has won armfuls of awards since its debut. The Italian beef sandwich is basically a co-star of the series.

The resolution, passed in Springfield, described The Bear as "a powerful and authentic portrayal of Chicago's food culture, its immigrant working-class roots, and the spirit of resilience, creativity, and excellence that defines the people of Illinois." It also credited the show with introducing the Italian beef sandwich "to new generations of fans across the globe" and called the sandwich an "ambassador of Illinois culture far beyond the state's borders." The resolution traces the sandwich's origins to working-class Italian immigrant communities in Chicago in the early 20th century. A food truck was parked outside the capitol building and served complimentary Italian beef sandwiches to celebrate.

The Bear actor Corey Hendrix, who grew up in the North Lawndale neighborhood of Chicago and plays Gary "Sweeps" Woods on the show, was at the state capitol in Springfield alongside State Rep. Rick Ryan and State Rep. Dave Vella for the ceremony. Hendrix received a framed copy of the resolution to take back to his bosses at FX and Disney. He kept it simple on the day: "I'm a Chicagoan. I love Italian beef. It's a really, really big deal."

The timing is deliberate and delicious. The Bear's fifth and final season premieres on June 25, 2026 on FX and Hulu. The season picks up the morning after the restaurant's crew discovers that head chef Carmy, played by Jeremy Allen White, has quit the food industry, leaving Sydney, Richie, and Natalie to run The Bear on their own. No money, a looming sale threat, and a storm bearing down. One last service. One last shot at a Michelin star. If Illinois just made the Italian beef official, the show is ending at exactly the right moment.
keyPoints: Illinois officially named the Italian beef sandwich the state sandwich on May 20, 2026
The resolution directly credits The Bear with bringing the sandwich to global attention
The Bear actor Corey Hendrix attended the ceremony in Springfield and received a framed copy of the resolution
The Italian beef replaces the Horseshoe as the state's official sandwich
The Bear's fifth and final season premieres June 25 on FX and Hulu

articles.title: SpaceX Put Grok's Sexualized Images Problem in Its IPO Filing
articles.summary: SpaceX has flagged xAI's Grok chatbot as a legal liability in its IPO paperwork, specifically citing complaints that Grok generated sexualized images. The rocket company has set aside more than $500 million to cover potential litigation losses tied to the issue. It is a rare moment where one Elon Musk company's AI problems are formally documented as a financial risk inside another Musk company's public filing.
articles.content:
SpaceX wants to go public, and its IPO filing is already turning heads — not just for the rockets, but for what it says about Grok. The filing names Grok's so-called "spicy" mode as a real legal risk, pointing to complaints that the xAI chatbot generated sexualized images. That is the kind of disclosure that tends to make investors sit up straight.

To cover its bases, SpaceX has set aside more than $500 million for potential litigation losses. Grok's image controversy is listed as one of the reasons that reserve exists. It is a significant sum, and it signals that SpaceX's legal team takes the exposure seriously enough to put a number on it.

The whole situation is a bit tangled. SpaceX and xAI are separate companies, but both sit inside the wider Elon Musk empire. When xAI's Grok runs into trouble, that trouble can ripple outward. Having Grok's "spicy" mode show up in a rival company's regulatory paperwork makes that ripple effect very visible and very public.

For anyone watching the Musk business universe, this is a notable moment. IPO filings are legal documents that have to tell the truth about risks. The fact that SpaceX's lawyers felt Grok's content issues were serious enough to disclose — and to back with half a billion dollars in reserves — says something about how real those concerns are considered to be.
keyPoints: SpaceX named Grok's sexualized image complaints as a legal risk in its IPO filing
SpaceX has set aside more than $500 million for potential litigation losses partly because of Grok
Grok's 'spicy' mode is the specific feature at the center of the complaints
SpaceX and xAI are separate companies but both are part of Elon Musk's broader business network
IPO filings are legal disclosures, making this one of the most formal acknowledgments of Grok's content problems yet

articles.title: FIFA Cancelled 70% of Reserved Rooms, Now World Cup Hotels Sit Empty
articles.summary: Eight out of ten US hotels in FIFA World Cup 2026 host cities say bookings are running below expectations, and FIFA is being blamed for cancelling up to 70% of its reserved room blocks. The move has left hotels scrambling with excess inventory just weeks before the tournament kicks off. It is a stunning reversal for venues that banked on the World Cup being a once-in-a-generation windfall.
articles.content:
The FIFA World Cup 2026 is supposed to be the biggest sporting event ever staged on US soil, spread across 16 host cities in the United States, Canada, and Mexico. Hotels signed up years in advance, held enormous room blocks for FIFA and its official partners, and turned away regular business to make room. Now those bets are looking very shaky.

A new survey has found that 80% of US hotels across World Cup host cities say bookings are running below expectations. The chief culprit? FIFA itself, which has cancelled up to 70% of the room blocks it originally reserved. That means hotels are sitting on huge amounts of empty inventory with no clear pipeline of fans to fill it.

The way room blocks work is simple. FIFA reserves large batches of rooms through official hospitality programmes, then sells packages to fans, sponsors, and delegates. When FIFA cancels those blocks, it is not just a headache for the hotels. It also signals that the official ticketing and hospitality machine is not moving product the way it expected to. Fans who were waiting for official packages may now be scrambling for alternatives, or simply not coming at all.

For the host cities, the stakes are enormous. Local economies were counting on a surge of visitor spending on food, transport, and entertainment, not just hotel beds. If the occupancy numbers do not recover fast, the World Cup 2026 could end up being a logistical embarrassment as much as a sporting celebration. FIFA has not yet offered a public explanation for the scale of the cancellations.
keyPoints: 80% of US World Cup host city hotels say bookings are below expectations
FIFA cancelled up to 70% of its originally reserved hotel room blocks
Hotels held the rooms for years and turned away other business to accommodate FIFA
The shortfall threatens the wider economic boost host cities were counting on
FIFA has not publicly explained the scale of the cancellations

articles.title: Radio Caroline Accidentally Announced King Charles Was Dead
articles.summary: Radio Caroline, the legendary UK station with pirate radio roots, mistakenly triggered its 'Death of a Monarch' emergency protocol on Tuesday afternoon and announced that King Charles III had died. A computer error at the station's studio in Maldon, Essex set off the automated procedure, which caused the station to play 'God Save the King' and go silent. The blunder happened while King Charles was very much alive and visiting Northern Ireland.
articles.content:
Radio Caroline has been making waves since 1964, and on Tuesday, 19 May, it made waves for all the wrong reasons. The iconic UK station accidentally announced that King Charles III had died. Not a joke, not a drill. The full death protocol fired off live on air.

Here is what actually happened. Due to a computer error at Radio Caroline's main studio, the Death of a Monarch procedure was accidentally activated on Tuesday afternoon, mistakenly announcing that King Charles had passed away. This is not a made-up contingency plan. The 'Death of a Monarch' procedure is something all UK stations hold in readiness while hoping never to require.

Listeners heard the station suspend regular shows "as a mark of respect" and play the national anthem 'God Save the King', after which the broadcast fell silent for approximately 15 minutes before normal service resumed. The timing made it all the more jarring. The incident occurred while King Charles was actually visiting Northern Ireland.

Radio Caroline is no stranger to controversy. Established in 1964 to challenge the BBC's broadcasting monopoly, Radio Caroline previously operated from ships off the English coast. After legislation in 1967 forced many pirate broadcasters to close, it continued intermittently before ending offshore broadcasts in 1990. Station manager Peter Moore issued a swift apology on Facebook. Radio Caroline then fell silent as would be required, which alerted staff to restore programming and issue an on-air apology, with Moore adding: "Caroline has been pleased to broadcast the King's Christmas message and we hope to do so for many years to come. We apologise to HM the King and to our listeners for any distress caused."
keyPoints: Radio Caroline accidentally triggered its Death of a Monarch emergency protocol on 19 May
A computer error at the studio in Maldon, Essex set off the automated broadcast
Listeners heard 'God Save the King' and about 15 minutes of silence before normal programming resumed
King Charles was alive and visiting Northern Ireland when the announcement aired
Station manager Peter Moore personally apologised to the King on Facebook
